Add an intermediary entity only if a specific documentary source establishes it.
openQuestions.question: Did Furnivall transcribe every Alexius witness directly, or did one or more versions reach him through Horstmann transcripts or prior prints?

next_steps: Consult the Gameson catalog PDF interactively or request the relevant description from Trinity Special Collections.
Ask Trinity whether a complete digital surrogate or reproduction can be supplied.
notes: The witness starts at f. 73, and Furnivall-derived milestones run through f. 77v; only the exact endpoint is unresolved.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact current-catalog endpoint of the Trinity College Oxford MS 57 Alexius witness, and is a complete public facsimile available?

next_steps: Compare DIMEV's counting convention against the edition and Laud 622 line by line.
Check whether DIMEV excludes omitted, shared, or differently lineated passage segments.
openQuestions.question: Why does DIMEV describe Furnivall's Old Testament History extract as 318 lines when the XML has 296 plus 64 lines, totaling 360?

next_steps: Compare the DIMEV witness locators and first/last-line data directly with Laud 622 and Furnivall pp. 92-96.
Retain the current explicit parent-child boundary unless stronger evidence requires revision.
openQuestions.question: Does DIMEV record 4622 intend its Laud 622 witness to cover only the 22-line Fifteen Signs opening or the full 54-line manuscript-continuous Signs plus Lamentacio sequence?

next_steps: Request target-locus reproductions through the Bodleian mediated copying service.
Recheck Digital Bodleian if the one-slide object is expanded.

next_steps: Keep reuse claims scoped to the exact passages published in 1878.
Obtain repository permission and specialist legal review before distributing unprinted variants or new photography.

object.description: The individual work traditions and witnesses represented in the target selection

reviewNotes: The cataloging subject is Furnivall's bounded five-work selection, not a single medieval work, the whole printed volume, or the whole Laud 622 manuscript.
The Life of St Alexius is one abstract hagiographic work represented by four version branches and six complete witness streams in the edition.
The two Solomon passages are non-contiguous excerpts from one Old Testament History; the later-printed 64-line passage physically precedes the 296-line passage in Laud 622.
Lamentacio animarum is retained as a manuscript-continuous child of Fifteen Signs rather than promoted to an independent title-level work.
The Conception of Mary material is a separately identifiable prologue-only witness: 86 XML long-line elements corresponding to 172 printed short-line positions.
Furnivall attributes only the Five Dreams to Adam Davy. Jerome is neither normalized as author nor inherited from Internet Archive metadata.
No intermediary transcript entity is asserted: Furnivall acknowledges Horstmann's notice of multiple Alexius versions, but the exact path from the six manuscripts to his working copy is unresolved.
London is the edition's publication place only. Essex localization belongs to the Laud 622 scribal-language layer and is not a composition place for the five-work selection.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is the pinned CME XML for University of Michigan item AHA2711.0001.001, whose immediate verbal source is Frederick James Furnivall's 1878 EETS edition. The edition-defined selection is not one medieval work: it contains five intellectual roots, while its Life of St Alexius root prints four versions in six witness streams. Furnivall transcribed the Five Dreams, the first Alexius version, both reordered Old Testament History excerpts, the manuscript-continuous Fifteen Signs and Lamentacio, and the separate Conception of Mary prologue from Bodleian MS Laud Misc. 622; the other five Alexius streams come from Cotton Titus A XXVI, the Vernon Manuscript, Laud Misc. 108, Laud Misc. 463, and Trinity College Oxford MS 57. Complete IIIF bundles are cached for Vernon and Laud 108. Only selected non-target images are public for Laud 622 and Laud 463, and no complete public surrogate was found for Cotton or Trinity 57. Physical witnesses, edition, scan, catalog data, U-M encoding, and repository serialization remain distinct rights and access layers.

catalogSummary.author: Adam Davy (Five Dreams only); anonymous Middle English authors for the remaining works
catalogSummary.date: Five Dreams composed during Edward II's reign; edition-defined selection published 1878
catalogSummary.region: Component composition places unresolved; Furnivall's edition was published in London; the Laud 622 scribal language is localized to Essex at the witness layer only
catalogSummary.title: Adam Davy's Five Dreams about Edward II and Other Middle English Verse (Furnivall's 1878 selection)

catalogingSubject.label: Adam Davy's Five Dreams about Edward II and Other Middle English Verse (Furnivall's 1878 selection)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the edition-defined Middle English verse selection represented by aha2711, not one medieval work and not the complete physical manuscript or printed book. It contains five intellectual works: Adam Davy's Five Dreams; the Life of St Alexius represented by four versions in six manuscript streams; two non-contiguous excerpts from one Old Testament History; the Fifteen Signs before Doomsday with its manuscript-continuous Lamentacio animarum; and the separately identifiable Conception of Mary prologue beginning Off ioye & blisse. Furnivall's front matter, notes, index, and other back matter are outside the cataloged unit even where the XML preserves omission markers or editorial framing.

contentParts.label: Adam Davy's Five Dreams about Edward II

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem'][1]; 166 intrinsic L in 4 LG; printed pp. 11-16

contentParts.summary: One 166-line poem containing five dream episodes, not five independently counted works; DIMEV record 5993 identifies the unique Laud 622 witness.

contentParts.label: First dream: Edward attacked before St Edward's shrine

locators.value: Five Dreams target L 1-42, excluding nested NOTE1 quotations

contentParts.summary: Target L positions 1-42; the attack is followed by colored bands springing from the king's ears.

contentParts.label: Second dream: Edward rides barefoot toward Rome
contentParts.summary: Target L positions 43-66, introduced as Anoþer sweuene.

contentParts.label: Third dream: imperial coronation at Rome
contentParts.summary: Target L positions 67-86, explicitly introduced as þe þrid sweuene.

contentParts.label: Fourth dream: Christ accompanies his knight
contentParts.summary: Target L positions 87-134; this dream shares an XML LG container with the third dream, so the textually signaled boundary is preserved without inventing an XML division.

contentParts.label: Fifth dream: the angel and the bleeding lamb
contentParts.summary: Target L positions 135-166, followed by Adam Davy's command to report the vision.

contentParts.label: Life of St Alexius: four versions from six manuscripts

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='legend']; printed pp. 17-80; 4,069 L in 513 LG across six streams
four versions from six manuscripts, printed in parallel on pp. 20-79

contentParts.summary: One abstract hagiographic work represented by four Middle English version branches and six complete witness streams; the parent is counted once and all version/stream children have item_count 0.

contentParts.label: Version 1: twelve-line-stanza Laud 622 version
contentParts.summary: DIMEV record 382; one witness stream in Bodleian MS Laud Misc. 622.

contentParts.label: Version 2: couplet Cotton Titus A XXVI version
contentParts.summary: DIMEV record 379; one witness stream in British Library Cotton MS Titus A XXVI.

contentParts.label: Version 3: earlier six-line-stanza version

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='legend']/DIV2[3]; 1,224 L in 204 LG across two streams

contentParts.summary: DIMEV record 4923; Furnivall prints the Vernon and Laud 108 streams in parallel.

contentParts.label: Vernon Manuscript stream
contentParts.summary: The complete 612-line Vernon copy of the earlier six-line-stanza version.

contentParts.label: Laud Misc. 108 stream
contentParts.summary: The complete 612-line Laud 108 copy of the earlier six-line-stanza version.

contentParts.label: Version 4: later six-line-stanza version

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='legend']/DIV2[4]; 1,271 L in 212 LG across two streams

contentParts.summary: DIMEV record 3102; Furnivall prints Laud 463 and Trinity College Oxford MS 57 in parallel.

contentParts.label: Laud Misc. 463 stream
contentParts.summary: The 635-line Laud 463 copy of the later six-line-stanza version.

contentParts.label: Trinity College Oxford MS 57 stream

locators.value: begins f. 73; XML milestones through f. 77v; endpoint unverified

contentParts.summary: A 636-line stream beginning at f. 73; Furnivall's milestones run through f. 77v, but the exact current-catalog endpoint remains unverified.

contentParts.label: Old Testament History selection

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem'][2] and BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='fragment']; printed pp. 81-90 and 96-98
f. 69 col. 1 and ff. 69v-70v within the larger imperfect Old Testament History at ff. 65r-70v

contentParts.summary: One work, DIMEV record 6345, represented by two Laud 622 excerpts that Furnivall separates and reorders: a 296-line continuation beginning with Solomon's counsel and a preceding 64-line Solomon passage appended later in the edition.

contentParts.label: King Solomon's Book of Wisdom
contentParts.summary: Furnivall's excerpt title for a 296-line portion that continues beyond Solomon through Rehoboam, Elijah, Elisha, Daniel, Doom, and Antichrist; it is not an autonomous biblical book authored by Solomon.

contentParts.label: Solomon's Coronation, Deeds, Judgment, Court, and Temple
contentParts.summary: A 64-line passage that physically precedes the Book of Wisdom excerpt in Laud 622 but is moved to pp. 96-98 by Furnivall; a still earlier birth passage is deliberately omitted.

contentParts.label: Fifteen Signs before Doomsday with Lamentacio animarum

locators.value: BODY/DIV1[@TYPE='poem'][3]/DIV2[1] and first 32 L of DIV2[2]; printed pp. 91-93

contentParts.summary: One manuscript-continuous eschatological unit of 54 long-line XML elements: the first 22 describe the Fifteen Signs and the following 32 continue through the Lamentacio rubric without a narrative break.

contentParts.label: Fifteen Signs before Doomsday
contentParts.summary: The opening 22 long lines, traditionally attributed to Jerome in the rubric and edition discussion but not cataloged as Jerome's authorship.

contentParts.label: Lamentacio animarum continuation
contentParts.summary: Thirty-two long lines following the rubric; Furnivall explicitly notes that the manuscript has no break in the story.

contentParts.label: Off ioye & blisse is my song: Conception of Mary prologue
contentParts.summary: A separately copied prologue-only witness identified by DIMEV record 4178. The XML has 86 long-line elements, each representing two printed short lines, or 172 printed short-line positions; DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 instead describes the work's added prologue generically as 84 lines.

assertion.notes: This date belongs to the edition-defined collection, not to composition of all medieval components.

dateStatements.display: Compiled as Furnivall's EETS selection and published in 1878
First published in this selection in 1878

assertion.notes: The internal address to Sir Edward the king supports the regnal range; the end-fourteenth-century manuscript date is not substituted for composition.

dateStatements.display: During the reign of Edward II, 1307-1327

extent.description: Five intellectual works are counted once at the root level. Their represented XML totals are 529 LG and 4,735 intrinsic L elements: Five Dreams 4/166; six Alexius witness streams 513/4,069; the two Old Testament History excerpts 6/360; Fifteen Signs plus Lamentacio 5/54; and the Conception prologue 1/86. The prologue's 86 XML L elements each contain two printed short lines, yielding 172 printed short-line positions; DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 describes the broader work as beginning with an 84-line prologue, so those source-specific conventions are not equated. The XML-wide 4,741 L total additionally includes six verse lines quoted inside editorial NOTE1 elements; those are excluded. Child dreams, version branches, streams, and excerpt portions have item_count 0 so they do not inflate the five-work count. The 17 P and 424 PB elements are editorial/carrier structures, not works; 124 PB REF values are unique.

formStatements.description: All five medieval intellectual works and all six Alexius streams are verse; editorial prose paragraphs and notes do not make the cataloged medieval selection mixed-form.

scope.description: Rubrics and editorially retained Latin headings

notes: Furnivall rejects the older attribution of all Laud 622 poems to Adam Davy; only Five Dreams is assigned to Davy here.
Saint Jerome's name is retained as a historical rubric/title feature, not normalized authorship; Furnivall himself notes that the Fifteen Signs text was not found in the Benedictine Jerome edition.
The Laud 622 end-fourteenth-century witness date and Essex dialect localization are witness-level facts, not shared composition metadata for all five works.
The complete Vernon and Laud 108 image bundles verify the exact Alexius loci, but image rights remain separate from the public-domain medieval text and edition layers.
All metrics are reproducible encoding counts and semantic boundary decisions, not a claim that XML L always equals one short verse line or that editorial parallelism creates additional works.

next_steps: Inspect Furnivall's surviving correspondence, transcripts, and EETS production papers.
Compare distinctive readings and errors against Horstmann's earlier Laud 108 printing before adding any intermediary derivation edge.
openQuestions.question: Did Furnivall transcribe every collateral Alexius stream directly from the physical manuscripts, or did any stream pass through Horstmann's transcripts or earlier print?

next_steps: Inspect the Gameson catalog record and physical or supplied images around ff. 73-78.
Reconcile Furnivall's milestones through f. 77v with DIMEV's start-only locator.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact current-foliation endpoint of the Trinity College Oxford MS 57 Alexius stream?

next_steps: Audit DIMEV's edition-locus/count convention against the 1878 printed lineation and its two non-contiguous excerpts.
openQuestions.question: Why does DIMEV report 318 edition lines for the Old Testament History selection while the two encoded excerpts contain 296 plus 64, or 360, intrinsic L elements?

next_steps: Compare DIMEV's record-construction rules and all witnesses of record 4622.
Preserve the XML and Furnivall boundary evidence rather than silently forcing either count.
openQuestions.question: Should DIMEV record 4622's 22-line description be read as only the Fifteen Signs opening even though its Laud 622 first/last lines and edition locus encompass the 54-line manuscript-continuous Signs plus Lamentacio?

next_steps: Compare the complete witnesses and DIMEV's line-count convention with Furnivall's printed numbering.
Keep the aha2711 encoding count and the DIMEV work-description count source-scoped meanwhile.
openQuestions.question: How should DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1's generic 84-line Conception prologue description be reconciled with the Laud 622 encoding's 86 long-line elements and 172 printed short-line positions?

next_steps: Ask the Bodleian mediated copying service about an internal surrogate or new photography of the target folios.
Request ff. 21v-27v and 69r-71v first, while distinguishing those target leaves from the whole codex.
openQuestions.question: Can Bodleian supply an existing complete digital surrogate of MS. Laud Misc. 622 beyond the single public 35mm slide?

assertion.notes: The attribution rests on repeated first-person self-naming within the unique Laud 622 witness and is accepted by the edition and DIMEV.

notes: Solomon and Saint Alexius are subjects, not authors; Furnivall reports but questions a traditional Jerome attribution for the Fifteen Signs, which is therefore not normalized as authorship.

assertion.notes: Furnivall states that the additional texts made the volume thick enough, selected six Alexius streams for parallel presentation, and moved the earlier Solomon passage after later manuscript material to complete his editorial Life of Solomon sequence.

subjects.label: The Fifteen Signs before the Day of Judgment

summaries.value: Furnivall's 1878 EETS selection brings together five distinct Middle English verse works: Adam Davy's politically prophetic Five Dreams; four versions of the Life of St Alexius printed from six manuscripts; selected Old Testament History passages centered on Solomon and later biblical history; the Fifteen Signs and its continuous Lamentacio; and a separately copied prologue to the Conception of Mary. The selection is cataloged as an editorial collection while preserving each work, version branch, witness stream, and non-contiguous excerpt as a scoped part.

The XML represents the medieval texts on printed pp. 11-98 and retains short editorial frames, but marks the half title, most front matter, and pp. 99-122 back matter as omitted. Its 124 unique page-image references are carrier links, while the 424 PB occurrences arise chiefly because the six Alexius streams are encoded in parallel. Six verse lines quoted inside editorial NOTE1 elements are excluded from the intellectual-work metrics.

assertion.notes: The qualifier makes the edition-defined mixed-work boundary explicit and is not part of the 1878 title.

titles.value: Adam Davy's Five Dreams about Edward II and Other Middle English Verse (Furnivall's 1878 selection)

notes: The title does not name the separate Off ioye & blisse song or the sixty-four-line Solomon fragment even though both are represented.

scope.description: Shortened transcription of the historical edition title

titles.value: Adam Davy's 5 Dreams about Edward II. The Life of St. Alexius. Solomon's Book of Wisdom. St. Jeremie's 15 Tokens before Doomsday. The Lamentation of Souls
Adam Davy's Five Dreams about Edward II

assertion.notes: DIMEV's work-level identification prevents Furnivall's excerpt title King Solomon's Book of Wisdom from being mistaken for an autonomous Solomon-authored book.

Conception of Mary (prologue: Off ioye & blisse is my song)

work.summary: Furnivall's 1878 EETS selection brings together five distinct Middle English verse works: Adam Davy's politically prophetic Five Dreams; four versions of the Life of St Alexius printed from six manuscripts; selected Old Testament History passages centered on Solomon and later biblical history; the Fifteen Signs and its continuous Lamentacio; and a separately copied prologue to the Conception of Mary. The selection is cataloged as an editorial collection while preserving each work, version branch, witness stream, and non-contiguous excerpt as a scoped part.
